I ran across this self-proclaimed copper bull on my feed, and the guy was literally wishing a death curse on the entire tech complex—EVs, AI, the whole nine yards.

Honestly, I was completely dumbfounded. I’ve been sitting on a massive book of copper equities for years now, but if you’re long Dr. Copper, you should be aggressively praying that Jensen Huang and Sam Altman don't get locked up and AI data centers don't spontaneously combust.

Legacy copper demand has been flatlining for a minute now. We've all heard the "China property sector is toast" macro tape until we're blue in the face. Granted this cycle is heavily supply-driven, but the demand side is anchored to the high tech narrative.

It's the energy transition and data centers that are holding up the structural demand thesis. This frontier demand is ramping on a hockey-stick trajectory; EVs were the headline act early on, but grid and transmission capex are rapidly becoming the real heavy lifters.

Backstopped by the AI revolution and this insatiable hunger for power, global grid capex is compounding exponentially, and copper is running the textbook picks and shovels play.

Also structurally speaking, an EV gobbles up almost 3x the copper intensity of a legacy ICE vehicle, meaning EVs already occupy a massive chunk of the demand pie chart.

To top it off AI data centers are more than double the copper-intensity of a vanilla crypto mining warehouse. The data center demand vector is moving in a straight line up and right.

And yet, some guys still want Tesla and Nvidia to go to zero? Give me a break. We need these tech nerds to keep printing.

Considering what was thrown at gold yesterday - surging oil and fuel prices, hawkish signals from the FOMC minutes, rising yields and traders once again entertaining the possibility of a rate hike - it has actually held up remarkably well. Street Signal | Agricultural prices face risks from food and energy security policies (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs reminds that global agricultural supply is highly concentrated in a small number of exporting countries. Export restrictions and biofuel policies implemented by these countries for their own food and energy security may amplify supply shocks and push up price fluctuations.

The report believes that protectionist policies are a source of upward risk for agricultural product prices. Recent market concerns include El Niño, biofuel mandates driven by high energy prices, and fertilizer shipment disruptions.

The underlying logic is that the market often overestimates fundamentals and underestimates the impact of policy disturbances on the agricultural product market. The hoarding and self-sufficiency strategies of importing countries will further fragment the market and reduce liquidity.

This geopolitically driven systemic risk premium may not be fully priced in by current markets. One-sentence conclusion: The securitization trend in the global agricultural market is replacing the pure logic of supply and demand and becoming the new dominant factor in agricultural product price fluctuations.

Good/bad: Good for agricultural product producers and traders (the four major ABCD grain merchants), but bad for food processing companies in developing countries that are highly dependent on imports. The market has not yet fully priced in the upward shift in the long-term price center brought about by policy risks. Catalysts:

1) Changes in export policies in major grain exporting countries (such as India and Argentina);

2) The impact of crude oil price trends on biofuel economics;

3) Weather conditions in major crop-producing areas.

Friday July 10 2026 | Early Read for the Overnight Play and into Tomorrow

The first good news of the week finally arrived. Trump said Iran called and wants to make a deal, and Qatar and Pakistan are pushing both sides back to the table. Oil cooled off, chips ripped, and SPX closed near the high of the day. But nothing is settled. Trump would not say whether the war is over, and the strikes have not stopped. Options for Friday are priced calm at ±35, the cheapest all week. Under that calm, traders sold their upside bets and kept their crash protection. They like this rally but they do not trust it yet. Friday is a quiet day on paper, with price likely to stay close to 7550. The one thing that can change that fast is an Iran headline.

📌 In the morning notes I said the day turns bullish above 7490. SPX opened at 7491 and slipped to 7481 in the first hour, right at the support I marked. From there price climbed the rest of the day, through 7500, then 7510, then 7525, and held above 7535. As I warned, 7550 has heavy resistance and it was not worth chasing. The high stopped at 7546.89, three points before it. Closed 7543.64.

THE VOL PICTURE

VIX fell 1.06 to 15.84 on the rally and most of that drop was mechanical. Price rolled up to a quieter part of the curve and Sticky Strike explains 0.77 of the move by itself. The curve barely changed.

The interesting part is what traders did at the edges. They sold the upside wings hard. And they kept the crash protection, the deep downside strikes actually got a little more bid on a day the market rallied on peace hopes. Read that again. On the day Iran said it wants a deal, nobody sold their hedges. The market wants the bounce but does not trust the story yet.

Thursday's Jobless Claims report came and went without any damage, and once it passed, near term options got much cheaper. The 9 day vol index dropped almost 2 points to 12.50 while the 30 day barely moved. Friday's straddle prices ±35 at 11.1 percent vol, down from 13.8 percent the day before, the cheapest day of the week. Options expiring Monday and even Tuesday now price single digit vol, while the market has actually been moving at closer to 15. And Tuesday morning is CPI plus five bank reports. Cheap options into the biggest morning of the month. One more thing about cheap options. If a bad headline hits, protection costs very little right now. And when everyone is priced for a quiet day, a surprise move runs further than normal.

WHAT HAPPENED THURSDAY

SPX closed 7543.64, up 60.93 points. Opened at 7491, dipped to 7481 early, and then climbed all day. Nasdaq rose 1.3 percent, the Dow added 0.3 percent. The engine was chips and the fuel was diplomacy. Trump said Iran called to make a deal, Qatar and Pakistan are working to get both sides talking, and oil cooled. Jobless Claims came and went without damage.

Chips carried everything. Lumentum rose 11 percent, HPE almost 10, ARM 9, and Micron added 5 percent after announcing billions more in US chip investment. Overnight the rally went global. The Kospi jumped more than 4 percent, the Nikkei rose 2.4 percent, and SoftBank surged 11 percent. Costco fell 4 percent. Gold just finished its worst quarter since 2013.

The Fed story moved too. Warsh named his task force members, and betting markets now put better than even odds on a rate hike in 2026 with the committee split. That is the backdrop CPI walks into on Tuesday.

For the week so far, SPX is up 0.8 percent, the Nasdaq 1.5 percent, and the Dow is down 0.8 percent. US futures are slightly lower overnight and Asia is broadly higher.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR FRIDAY

The biggest options position of the day sits at 7550, by a wide margin, and price closed six points under it. When one strike is this much bigger than everything else around it, price tends to get pulled toward that strike and stay near it, especially into the afternoon. Options pricing agrees, it puts Friday's most likely close between 7552 and 7570. 7500 is also heavy on both the call side and the put side, which makes it the strong support under the day. So the likely range is 7500 to 7550. Between those two strikes there is not much positioned, so price crosses the middle quickly and the real reactions happen at the edges.

The upside path is simple. If price breaks above 7575 and 7580, there is not much resistance until 7610, so the move can run. Someone has already made a large bet that the market stays above 7580. If this plays out, take it one level at a time. Do not jump ahead, and do not buy far away strikes hoping for a home run. Heavy sellers are sitting at 7620, the biggest position of the day. With chips rallying around the world, this scenario is real.

On the downside, a slip below 7540 moves fast at first because of the bought puts sitting under spot. But 7530 and 7525 carry two of the biggest open positions of the day. A first dip usually slows there and often turns back up toward 7550. If sellers keep pressing and those levels break, 7510 is thin and the drop speeds up again until 7500 catches it. Below 7500, 7490 is a thin pocket and the big open position at 7475 speeds drops up, so it moves fast until the 7460 to 7465 support zone, which also has the 20 day average at 7464. Then 7450. The trapdoor at 7425 is rebuilt. This is the war headline path, and it only takes one post to open it.

Quiet day, cheap options, one pin, two tails. Trade the range until a headline picks a tail.

Street Signal | Costco’s sales in June were slightly lower than expected, and customer traffic growth slowed down (Goldman Sachs)

Goldman Sachs released a commentary on Costco's June sales data. The core change is that the growth rate of same-store sales (core comparable, excluding oil prices and exchange rates) in June did not meet expectations, and the growth of passenger flow decelerated.

Specific data shows that global core comparable sales growth in June may be lower than market expectations of 6.0%, and the conversion rate from GMV to comparable sales is also under pressure.

The logic behind it is that in a high inflation and interest rate environment, consumers are becoming more cautious in their purchasing behavior, and the amount of a single purchase is affected. Although Costco has defensive attributes with its unique membership model, the transmission of weak macro consumption is emerging.

The market's previous expectations for Costco's continued higher-than-expected growth may have been too high, and the slowdown in monthly sales data is an important warning signal. When "cheapness" has also become a pain point for Costco, the membership giant is feeling the aftermath of the "consumption downgrade" storm.

One sentence conclusion: Even retail fortresses like Costco are beginning to show signs of weakness in the face of consumption headwinds - this cold air has just begun to spread from the United States to the world. Positive/negative: Neutral to negative for Costco (https://t.co/Wug5Y2wqyT).

The market has high expectations that Costco will continue to outperform its peers, and slowing sales may trigger a revaluation. Catalysts:

1) Monthly sales data in July and subsequent months;

2) The company’s 4Q FY26 (as of the end of August) financial report held in August;

3) Macroeconomic data such as US CPI and consumer confidence index.

《LinkedIn 2026 Talent Acquisition Trends Report: Surge in Applications, AI Adoption, and Trust Reconstruction in Global Recruitment》

The report was commissioned by LinkedIn to Censuswide, surveying 6,554 HR professionals across 13 global markets plus the Middle East and North Africa (Saudi Arabia, UAE) from November 10 to 27, 2025. Censuswide is a member of ESOMAR’s UK Polling Council. The methodology section clearly defines three core metrics: Recruitment Rate(share of LinkedIn members in a country adding new employer information to their profiles in a given month, divided by the country’s total member count, calculated as a three-month moving average from October 2019 to October 2025), Hiring Quality(tenure ≥12 months plus meeting at least one of the following: high demand in the market, internal mobility, or manager-level placement), and InMail Reply Rate(rolling 28-day active member count, replies within 3 days, controlling for interaction rates). Core findings: Country-level recruitment rates turned positive for India (+40%) and the UAE (+37%), while turning negative for Brazil (-5%), Germany (-17%), Mexico (-20%), Australia (-21%), Singapore (-21%), Canada (-22%), the US (-23%), the UK (-25%), France (-30%), and the Netherlands (-35%). Sector-wise, healthcare (+18%), education (+8%), and consumer services (+7%) posted positive rates, while oil & gas (-45%), professional services (-29%), tech/media/information (-27%), and manufacturing (-27%) ranked at the bottom. 52% of job seekers plan to look for new roles in 2026; 66% of recruiters say finding qualified candidates grew harder over the past year, 52% cite a shortage of qualified talent, 42% face pressure to fill roles faster, and 39% are pressured to tap into “hidden talent pools.”

The report’s core narrative follows three threads. First, the paradox of “surging applications amid scarce qualified talent”: Global job application volume has risen sharply relative to the July 2019 median, yet recruiters struggle to filter through high volumes rather than select confidently, attributed to the “failure of traditional signals (job titles, keywords, tenure).” Second, skill evolution and AI adoption: 38% of skills required for global jobs changed between 2016 and 2023, a figure projected to jump to 70% by 2030 driven by generative AI; 1 in 10 new hires in 2026 will hold a job title that did not exist in 2000. 93% of recruiters plan to increase AI use in 2026, targeting discovery of candidates missed by legacy methods, deeper skill insights, process acceleration, and simplified initial screening. Teams using AI hire 17% more high-potential talent, and companies with “higher-quality hiring growth” post revenue growth rates 2x faster than peers (LinkedIn data, October 2025). Third, trust and compliance: Over 100 million members and 700,000 recruiters are now verified; more than 50% of job postings are verified, accounting for over 80% of LinkedIn job view traffic. Verified members see 27% higher InMail reply rates than unverified peers; when 75%+ of a company’s recruiters are verified, InMail acceptance rates rise another 17%. The compliance section lists the EU AI Act, GDPR/CCPA, and local labor laws, outlining a 5-step action plan: early cross-functional intervention, clear definition of AI use cases, asking vendors targeted questions, regulatory awareness, and structured change management.

Testimonials from 6 Chinese enterprises expanding globally occupy 4 pages: Elaine Zhang of Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University breaks roles into three tiers: strategic core roles (6–12 months of global targeted sourcing + AI background checks), high-potential roles (3–6 months of targeted outreach to PhD and postdoctoral fellows), and administrative roles (15–20 working days of structured recruitment). Jiang Feng of NIO highlights its values (sincerity, care, foresight, action) plus VAU/VI/EI systems and hardware-software synergy (SkyOS, Tianxing chassis) to break traditional automaker barriers. Ren Zhe of Milink positions “talent + AI” as a composite strategic asset. Jerry Ye of Whale advocates shifting from JD-centric recruitment to “skill tags + AI competency profiling,” focusing on LLMs, agents, RAG, AI infrastructure, and global collaboration. Wu Minhui of Jack Sewing Machine emphasizes “skill tags + holistic talent profiling” to overcome cross-regional barriers. Liu Fang of Haoyuan Pharma outlines a workflow: clarify organizational needs → tier recruitment channels → boost efficiency via AI (resume tagging and multilingual screening via ChatGPT/Gemini) → conduct structured interviews to assess motivation and values. All six are LinkedIn clients, so their testimonials are vendor-provided rather than independent case studies. The report closes with LinkedIn product promotions (Recruiter Assistant, AI Academy, 2026 Global Talent Productivity Report).

【Keywords】: #LinkedIn #2026TalentTrends #Censuswide #ESOMAR #RecruitmentRate #SurgeInApplications #ScarcityOfQualifiedCandidates #AIRecruitment #GenerativeAI #SkillShift #38Percent #70Percent #HighPotentialTalent #HiringQuality #VerifiedMembers #EUAIAct #GDPR #CCPA #XiAnJiaotongLiverpoolUniversity #NIO #Milink #Whale #JackSewingMachine #HaoyuanPharma #GlobalRecruitmentForChineseEnterprises #EmployerBranding #LinkedInVerification #StructuredInterview #SkillTags #HumanAICollaboration #WhiteCollarRecruitment

【Perspective】:
The conservative perspective​ prioritizes compliance risks and sample bias. As a LinkedIn-commissioned report, its methodology defines metrics clearly but the sample of 6,554 HR professionals—all LinkedIn users across 13 markets plus MENA—naturally skews toward digitally mature enterprises already using LinkedIn, excluding small manufacturers, blue-collar dispatch agencies, and offline local recruiters. These segments form the core of the HRO and dispatch market covered in the earlier Shuoyuan report, meaning the “recruitment market” described here does not overlap with that analysis. Conservatives flag the EU AI Act’s impact on AI recruiting tools: Starting in 2026, the EU classifies recruitment algorithms as “high-risk AI systems,” mandating algorithmic transparency, data governance, and human oversight. If LinkedIn’s AI Recruiter Assistant is used to screen EU candidates, it faces dual compliance hurdles: GDPR-regulated cross-border resume data transfers and the EU AI Act’s Article 13–15 requirements for explaining automated decisions. The report’s 5-step compliance plan only mentions “understanding regulations” without detailing how LinkedIn’s products will adapt, leaving a gap for Chinese enterprises expanding into Europe (NIO, Jack Sewing Machine, Haoyuan Pharma all have EU operations). Another conservative anchor: The claim that “93% of recruiters plan to increase AI use in 2026” reflects stated intent, not actual behavior. Classic intention-behavior gaps range from 30–50%, so actual adoption rates by end-2026 will likely fall within the 50–70% range.

The neutral perspective​ examines the reverse mechanism behind the “application surge” and flaws in causal claims about AI adoption. The report notes “surging applications and 66% of recruiters struggling to find qualified talent” but fails to break down application composition: Generative AI enables job seekers to mass-submit tailored resumes and cover letters via “one-click applications,” meaning much of the volume surge is AI-generated noise. This creates a two-sided platform “AI arms race”: job seekers use AI to boost submission volume → recruiters use AI to boost screening volume → platform traffic inflates while signal density declines. The report only addresses recruiter pain points without explaining applicant-side drivers, resulting in one-sided attribution. Further, the claim that “AI-using teams hire 17% more hi-po talent and drive 2x faster revenue growth” reflects correlation rather than causation. Teams adopting AI typically have larger budgets, more mature processes, and stronger employer brands—hi-po hiring and revenue growth may stem from team quality rather than AI use. The report does not control for variables like company size, sector, or region before comparing AI adopters to non-adopters, making the “compound return” claim overly marketing-laden. Two neutral takeaways hold value: First, Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University’s three-tiered role categorization (strategic/high-potential/administrative) provides a replicable framework to balance speed, quality, and depth—extending the “standardized processes for large enterprises vs. flexible customization for SMEs” logic from the Shuoyuan HRO report. Second, verification system data (100M+ verified members, 700k+ verified recruiters, verified postings driving 80%+ of job views, 27% higher InMail reply rates) is transparently sourced from LinkedIn’s platform and offers actionable insights for employer branding.

The radical perspective​ situates this report within the broader AI capex chain analyzed in prior rounds: Goldman Sachs’ 250–300% DRAM price hike → SK Hynix ADR oversubscription → CXMT IPO (hard tech R&D roles) → Meta’s $145B Capex / 7–14GW compute buildout (AI infrastructure roles) → AAOI/SIVERS/AXTI (optical R&D roles) → industrial microgrids (high-energy facility O&M roles) → Shuoyuan HRO (blue-collar dispatch roles). This LinkedIn report maps to the “hard tech R&D + overseas white-collar roles” segment: CXMT needs DDR5/HBM engineers (aligning with the 6–12 month strategic role logic), Meta needs MTIA/compute talent (requiring global targeted sourcing), and Chinese exporters like NIO/Jack/Milink need cross-cultural “hardware-software collaborative” talent. Crucially, these roles fall into the worst-performing sectors in the report: tech (-27%), manufacturing (-27%), and professional services (-29%)—creating a sharp contrast with the broader application surge. Scarce roles (AI/hard tech/cross-border composites) face persistent shortages even as low-value roles see application flooding, reflecting K-shaped divergence rather than universal market decline. Radicals also identify verification as the next competitive moat: LinkedIn’s push for verified members/postings is fundamentally a hedge against AI-generated spam and fake applications (the report notes 1/3 of recruiters struggle to distinguish genuine applications). Platforms that build a tripartite closed loop (candidate verification + skill verification + employer verification) will capture the second-wave dividend of AI recruiting—not just LinkedIn, but domestic platforms like BOSS Zhipin and Liepin if they adopt enterprise authentication + real-user verification + skill tagging frameworks.

Abstracting two levels higher, the report is a four-in-one marketing vehicle combining LinkedIn platform data, client testimonials, AI recruiting tool promotion, and verification product positioning. Its strengths include transparent metric definitions, ESOMAR-aligned sampling, and granular country/sector data. Weaknesses include the unsupported causal chain linking “93% AI adoption intent → 17% more hi-po hires → 2x revenue growth” (confounding correlation with causation + intention-behavior gaps), biased vendor testimonials, and selection bias toward LinkedIn-active HR professionals. Decision-making implications split into three tiers: For HR practitioners, the three-tiered role framework and verification system are replicable, but avoid FOMO over the 93% adoption statistic—run small-scale POCs first to validate whether the 17% hi-po hiring lift applies to your sector. For enterprises expanding globally, the skill-tag + AI profiling approach is sound, but pre-build EU AI Act/GDPR compliance frameworks to avoid regulatory pitfalls in 2026–2027. For investors, the report’s -27% tech recruitment rate and Shuoyuan’s “hundreds of billions in blue-collar HRO revenue” reflect two entirely separate markets: white-collar professional roles (LinkedIn’s coverage) face slowing growth plus AI-driven disruption, while blue-collar dispatch (Shuoyuan’s coverage) benefits from policy support, new energy demand, and globalization. Within the HR sector, Career International, Reruit Group, FSG, and Beijing FESCO operate across three distinct beta exposures: hard tech R&D outsourcing (CXMT-type), blue-collar dispatch (industrial microgrid-type), and overseas white-collar recruitment (LinkedIn-type)—each requiring separate valuation.

core PCE growth rate will drop from the current 3.4% to 3.0% in December 2026, and the core CPI will drop from the current 2.9% to 2.6%. The core CPI month-on-month readings in the coming months are expected to be moderate.

Meta’s self-developed AI chip “Iris” will be mass-produced as soon as September, and its computing power is expected to double to 14 gigawatts in 2027 Meta is accelerating its self-research strategy for chips in order to reduce its dependence on external suppliers such as Nvidia.

On July 9, according to an internal memo seen by Reuters, Meta plans to start mass production of its self-developed AI chip code-named "Iris" as soon as September this year, and plans to maintain an iterative rhythm of launching a new generation of chips approximately every six months until 2027.

At the same time, the company aims to double the total data center computing capacity to 14 gigawatts (GW) in 2027. This means that Meta is accelerating to get rid of its high dependence on Nvidia GPUs, reducing AI infrastructure costs through self-developed chips, and enhancing computing power autonomy.

The memorandum shows that the "Iris" chip completed key tests in just six weeks, and no major problems were found. The progress is significantly faster than the pace of similar chips in the industry that usually takes several months to verify, showing that Meta's self-research project has made a substantial breakthrough. As of press time, Meta's U.S.

stocks fell more than 2.5% before the market opened.

PepsiCo's Q2 revenue exceeds expectations, weak performance in North America drags down earnings The financial report shows that PepsiCo's Q2 net revenue increased 6.4% year-on-year to US$24.18 billion, which was better than the market expectation of US$23.95 billion; operating profit was US$4.02 billion, slightly lower than the US$4.06 billion expected by the market; core earnings per share was US$2.20, slightly better than the US$2.19 expected by the market.

Although revenue continued to grow, profit performance was slightly lacking. The core drag on performance comes from the North American market. The company's management pointed out that sales in the U.S. food and beverage sector have slowed, inflation is still squeezing household budgets, and consumers have to cut daily spending.

Specifically, snack sales in North America were basically flat, while beverage sales fell by 4%. In contrast to North America, overseas markets continue to provide support - the demand for snacks and beverages in the international market is relatively strong, driving overall snack sales to increase by 3% and beverage sales to increase by 2%.

This partially helped offset weakness in North America. In addition, the company still forecasts endogenous revenue growth of 2% to 4% for the full year of 2026, and the median forecast range of 3% is better than the market expectation of 2.76%.

Ahead of ASML’s Q2 results, Xiaomo shares are as high as $2,200: short-term performance is not the real test, and the outlook for 2027 is the key to revaluation Lithography giant ASML will announce its second quarter 2026 results on July 15.

Due to recent market concerns about the sustainability of artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditures, ASML's stock price has been relatively sluggish - its US stock price has fallen by more than 11% since July.

In a recently released research report, JPMorgan Chase previewed ASML's second-quarter performance and pointed out that the company needs to release signals of capacity expansion and strong demand in 2027 and beyond before the stock price is expected to achieve a breakthrough.

Regarding ASML's second-quarter performance, JPMorgan Chase expects the company's second-quarter revenue to reach 8.7 billion euros (a year-on-year increase of 13.1% and a month-on-month decrease of 0.8%), which is 1.4% lower than the market consensus forecast, but in line with the mid-point of the company's previously given guidance range of 8.4-9 billion euros.

In terms of profitability, JPMorgan Chase estimates that the company’s gross profit margin in the second quarter will be 51.7%, which is within the company’s guidance range (51%-52%) and slightly lower than the market consensus of 52%; second quarter earnings per share are expected to be 6.67 euros, 2.3% lower than the market consensus expectation.

ASML previously predicted that revenue in 2026 will increase by 16.3% year-on-year to approximately 38 billion euros (based on the mid-range value). Currently, the market's consensus expectation for ASML's revenue growth in 2026 is 19.8%, which is higher than the mid-point of the guidance range given by the company, but still within the guidance range.

JPMorgan Chase expects ASML to raise its 2026 performance guidance due to stronger-than-expected shipments of DUV lithography equipment.

Although JPMorgan Chase's forecast for ASML's second-quarter performance is lower than the market consensus, its analysis shows that short-term performance may not be of much significance to the stock price, and the real focus of the market will be 2027.

The bank believes that ASML's growth in 2027 is expected to be significantly higher than the overall growth of the global wafer fab equipment (WFE) market.

This cannot be achieved in 2026 because customer orders started late (starting in December 2025), resulting in the supply chain having no time to increase production capacity and therefore unable to deliver more EUV equipment in 2026.

JPMorgan Chase estimates ASML’s earnings per share in 2027 and 2028 to be 54.4 euros and 64.4 euros respectively, which are 26.5% and 23.8% higher than current market expectations respectively. (

ING: Le Pen returns to the 2027 French presidential election and financial risks remain a core variable in the market ING said in its latest report that Marine Le Pen, leader of the French far-right National Rally (RN), has regained the qualification to run in the 2027 presidential election, but its legal uncertainty, French political fragmentation and fiscal deterioration prospects will jointly increase the risk of election war and market volatility.

The report pointed out that Le Pen was found guilty in March 2025 of the European Parliament Assistant Fund Abuse Case. She was originally sentenced to 4 years in prison, with 2 years of probation, and was banned from holding public office for 5 years.

The Court of Appeal upheld the conviction, but adjusted the sentence to 3 years, 2 of which were suspended, and the other 1 year to be executed by electronic monitoring; the public office ban was adjusted to 45 months, of which 30 months were suspended.

Since the first 15 months of the ban have expired from March 2025, Le Pen can therefore participate in the 2027 presidential election. Le Pen has announced that she will run on behalf of the RN and said that if she wins, Jordan Bardera will be the prime minister.

However, she has decided to appeal to the Supreme Court, which is expected to rule in early 2027, and legal risks may still hang over the campaign. In terms of polls, the RN candidate's current support rate is about 35%, clearly ahead of the 15% to 20% of centrist candidates, and the 10% to 15% of left-wing candidates such as Mélenchon and Glucksmann.

ING believes that RN will most likely lead in the first round, but its opponents in the second round are still highly uncertain, depending on whether the left wing is integrated and whether the center is dominated by Edouard Philippe or Gabriel Attal.

At the policy level, RN has downplayed its proposals for "leaving the Eurozone" and a referendum on EU membership, and the market is less concerned about its potential victory than before.

However, the party still criticizes EU fiscal rules, advocates reducing production taxes and value-added taxes, has an unclear position on pensions, and lacks a clear plan on how to stabilize public debt in the long term. France's financial pressure will become the main theme of the campaign.

The government has lowered its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 0.7% from the budget assumption of 1.0%, and the target of reducing the deficit rate to 5.0% this year from 5.1% in 2025 is more difficult to achieve.

The European Commission estimates that if the policy remains unchanged, France's deficit rate will rise to 5.7% in 2027, and the public debt rate will reach 120.2%.

ING said that the bond market is more concerned about fiscal improvement than the candidates themselves, and the spread of 10-year French bonds relative to German bonds has returned to around 80 basis points; in the foreign exchange market, the euro against the Swiss franc will be the clearest indicator of political risk in France.

Goldman Sachs: The Fed's baseline scenario for the year is still to hold on to the inflation path and decide whether to raise or cut interest rates.

Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the minutes of the Federal Reserve’s June meeting showed that all FOMC participants supported maintaining the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, but a few participants believed that there was reason to raise interest rates at that time.

The minutes showed that the Fed's policy path is still highly dependent on inflation trends, rather than a unidirectional shift to easing or tightening. The research report stated that most participants discussed different macro scenarios.

If inflationary pressures subside soon and begin to return to near the 2% target, almost all relevant participants believe that keeping interest rates unchanged or eventually cutting interest rates may be appropriate options.

On the contrary, if inflation continues to be high due to AI-related demand, conflict in the Middle East, or the impact of tariffs, almost all relevant participants believe that further tightening of policy may be necessary. The minutes of the meeting showed that participants generally believed that U.S.

inflation has risen further and is significantly higher than the 2% target, due to tariffs, supply chain disruptions, and AI investment demand.

"Several" participants pointed out that price pressures have become broader, involving areas such as transportation, air tickets, petrochemicals and agricultural inputs; service sector inflation remains high after excluding housing.

Most participants also believed that AI-related investment demand pushes the economy to grow faster than potential, which may make inflation more sustainable. However, the labor market was seen as remaining stable, with many participants saying it was not currently a source of inflationary pressures.

Economic activity is still expanding at a steady pace, mainly supported by AI-related business investment; higher stock prices and previous tax rebates have also boosted consumption, especially spending by high-income households. Goldman Sachs predicts that the U.S.

core PCE growth rate will drop from the current 3.4% to 3.0% in December 2026, and the core CPI will drop from the current 2.9% to 2.6%. The core CPI month-on-month readings in the coming months are expected to be moderate.

The bank believes that although there are still risks of interest rate hikes this year, the baseline scenario is still for the Federal Reserve to maintain policy interest rates unchanged in 2026.

Goldman Sachs: Tensions in Hormuz may hinder the recovery of Middle East oil supplies Goldman Sachs estimated in a July 8 research report that crude oil production in the Persian Gulf region in June was still about 10.5 million barrels per day lower than pre-war levels.

Analysts at the bank pointed out that although Middle Eastern oil-producing countries have begun to restart previously shut down oil wells in the past month, if the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted again, it may slow down the recovery of production.

Goldman Sachs said recent attacks on oil tankers showed that the risk of crossing the Strait of Hormuz remains high. With the status of the ceasefire unclear, shipping companies may delay passage, suppressing short-term oil flows.

The bank estimates that Persian Gulf oil transportation volume has fallen back to about 70% of normal levels after the recent attacks on oil tankers; previously, in the first 10 days after the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, transportation volume once returned to more than 80% of pre-war levels.

Goldman Sachs believes that Persian Gulf oil flows and oil prices currently face two-way risks.

If negotiations continue in the next 60 days, shipping safety is guaranteed, and Iranian crude oil sales exemptions are renewed, transportation volume is expected to resume before the end of July; if negotiations fail and tanker attacks escalate, traffic may decline further.

Previously, with the resumption of navigation to Hormuz, Goldman Sachs had lowered its oil price forecast and warned that there is still a risk of oversupply reappearing in the crude oil market.

Goldman Sachs: Food and energy security policies may increase agricultural product price volatility Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that global agricultural supply is highly concentrated, with the top three exporters of key varieties such as corn, soybeans, rice, sugar and palm oil accounting for 60% to 90% of global trade volume, making the agricultural product market more vulnerable to local weather, geopolitical and policy shocks.

The bank believes that what really amplifies price fluctuations is not just the decline in production, but the protectionist measures taken by exporting countries to ensure domestic food and energy security.

Once there is a supply disturbance, or even just a disturbance in expectations, policies such as export restrictions, mandatory increases in biofuel blending ratios, and inventory hoarding may reduce exportable supply, reduce liquidity in the global trade market, and amplify price reactions.

Goldman Sachs mentioned that India accounts for about 40% of global rice exports. It banned the export of non-Basmati rice in 2023 due to concerns about El Niño. Although India's output ultimately remained resilient that year, rice prices in Asia rose significantly as a result.

Similarly, India recently restricted sugar exports due to the risk of a potential "super" El Niño. Energy security could also squeeze food supplies. From 2016 to 2026, Indonesia increased the blending ratio of biodiesel from 20% to 40% to 50%.

However, its palm oil production has limited growth due to land and yield constraints, resulting in a decline in exportable palm oil balances and pushing up the bottom of vegetable oil prices.

Goldman Sachs believes that agricultural product prices face three major upward risks in the future: El Niño has emerged and has a 63% probability of developing into a "super" El Niño; higher energy prices in the first half of 2026 may prompt more countries to increase their biofuel requirements; and if the Strait of Hormuz is disturbed again, it will affect the nitrogen fertilizer purchasing season in the third quarter.

The bank said that although protectionist policies are intended to enhance domestic resilience, they may exacerbate fragmentation and price volatility in global agricultural markets. In terms of Alphabet, Goldman Sachs emphasized that it is still one of the most leveraged companies in the AI theme and raised its Google Cloud revenue forecast.

It is expected to grow by 71% year-on-year in 2026 and 65% in 2027, reaching approximately US$400 billion by 2030, with a five-year compound growth rate of approximately 47%. However, demand for AI computing power continues to exceed supply, which has also pushed up capital expenditures.

Goldman Sachs estimates that Alphabet’s cumulative capital expenditures from 2026 to 2028 will be approximately US$790 billion; Meta’s cumulative capital expenditures during the same period will be approximately US$534 billion.

Goldman Sachs said that Meta’s exploration of monetizing idle computing power in the future does not mean that it is slowing down its investment in AI, but is more likely to take advantage of the mismatch between supply and demand in a phased manner.

The bank believes that the return on AI capital expenditure is still the core controversy that investors are most concerned about.

Goldman Sachs: Nearline HDD demand continues to be strong Goldman Sachs said in its latest research report that after holding an expert conference call with Techno Systems Research, it once again confirmed that the nearline HDD supply and demand pattern is still healthy and tight, and that demand for AI data centers and cloud storage will continue to support the prosperity of the HDD industry chain.

The bank maintains a "buy" rating on TDK and HOYA, and is optimistic about the benefit space of TDK in the field of HDD heads and suspensions and HOYA in the field of glass substrates respectively.

TSR predicts that the global HDD total addressable market (TAM) in 2026 will be 129.1 million units, a year-on-year increase of 4%; of which near-line HDD shipments are expected to reach 75.6 million units, a year-on-year increase of 12%, accounting for the main part of the overall HDD demand. The production end also remains high.

Total HDD production in 2026 is expected to be 134.9 million units, of which near-line HDD production is 84.37 million units, higher than 74.77 million units in 2025.

In terms of price, the research report pointed out that in 2026, HDD manufacturers will no longer rely solely on expanding near-line HDD shipments or production capacity, but will be more active in promoting single-machine capacity increases, thereby driving up the average unit price, and at the same time starting to increase prices on the basis of the same products.

Discussions on long-term supply agreements (LTA) for 2030 have already occurred in the industry, and Goldman Sachs expects the price increase trend to continue. Data shows that the overall HDD ASP is expected to rise from US$180.1 in 2025 to US$236.0 in 2026; industry revenue is expected to increase from US$22.291 billion to US$30.478 billion.

In terms of parts and components, Goldman Sachs said that the current material and component bottlenecks have eased than before, but some materials may still become constraints again in the future. Technological upgrades may drive up ASPs, but there is no clear trend yet in direct price increases for the same product.

Beneficiaries mainly include the increase in the number of components, the increase in unit price brought about by technology upgrades, and the increase in the near-line HDD market share of suppliers. Among them, TDK benefits from the increased demand for HDD head outsourcing.

According to the research report, starting from the April to June quarter, the proportion of Western Digital and Seagate outsourcing magnetic heads to TDK is expected to increase significantly.

TDK has previously disclosed that HDD magnetic head shipments are expected to increase by 40% in FY3/27, of which near-line HDD magnetic head shipments are expected to increase by 50%. Goldman Sachs gave TDK a 12-month target price of 4,600 yen and HOYA a target price of 33,000 yen.

Bank of America: Nvidia’s valuation has reflected excessively pessimistic expectations and maintains a buy rating In its latest research report, Bank of America maintained a "buy" rating for Nvidia with a target price of $350, believing that the market's concerns about its AI growth prospects have been amplified.

The research report pointed out that although Nvidia maintains its dominant position in the field of AI chips, its stock price rose only 3% during the year, significantly lagging behind the 82% increase of the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index.

The current forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18 times is at a seven-year low, and the valuation already implies a discount of approximately 30% to 35% to earnings per share from 2027 to 2028.

Bank of America believes that investors are mainly worried about four points: rising storage costs such as HBM compressing gross profit margins, competition from self-developed ASICs by cloud vendors, crowded institutional holdings, and NVIDIA's investment in suppliers and customers that may affect cash returns.

However, the bank judged that the market underestimated Nvidia’s pricing power, supply chain scale and full-stack ecological advantages.

On the cost side, Bank of America predicts that the cost of a single rack HBM will only increase by about US$200,000 to US$300,000 from Blackwell to Rubin, while the selling price of the entire rack may rise from US$3 million to US$4 million to US$6 million to US$7 million, which is enough to offset the storage cost pressure, and the gross profit margin is expected to remain in the mid-range of about 75%.

The company's supply chain advance commitments of more than $119 billion also help secure storage, wafer, packaging and power supplies. In terms of competition, Bank of America pointed out that Nvidia's GPU accelerator revenue has increased approximately 700 times since the launch of Google TPU in 2015.

Nvidia's recent sales to hyperscale cloud vendors have increased by 115% year-on-year, nearly twice the growth rate of cloud capital expenditures, showing that its share of cloud vendor wallets is still increasing. Bank of America predicts that Nvidia can maintain a share of more than 65% to 70% of AI capital expenditures in the long term.

Bank of America also predicts that the AI data center system market will increase from US$273 billion in 2025 to approximately US$1.7 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 44%; of which the AI accelerator market will reach approximately US$1.1 trillion.

Based on this assumption, Nvidia's earnings per share are expected to rise from more than $13 in 2027 to more than $25 in 2030. Risks include AI project fluctuations, gaming business cycles, export restrictions to China, slowing capital returns, and regulatory scrutiny.

Goldman Sachs raises Amazon target price to $335, saying AI investment cycle is still a mid- to long-term buying point Goldman Sachs maintained a "buy" rating in Amazon's second-quarter earnings forecast report and raised its 12-month target price to $335 from $325.

The bank believes that although Amazon’s stock price fluctuated and weakened this quarter, the core controversies surrounding consumers, AI capital expenditures, AWS growth, Trainium’s self-developed chips and smart shopping have not changed the logic of medium- and long-term revenue compound growth and profit margin expansion.

On the consumer side, Goldman Sachs believes that Amazon users as a whole are still stable. Recent Prime Day sales have increased by 9.5% year-on-year. However, the structure shows trends such as greater price sensitivity, consumption tilting towards necessities, and purchasing power being more concentrated in high-income households.

AWS is still the focus of research reports. Goldman Sachs raised its AWS revenue and capital expenditure forecasts due to factors including AI computing power demand continuing to exceed supply, backlog conversion, traditional workload migration, production capacity online, and some pricing adjustments starting from July 1.

The bank expects AWS revenue to grow by approximately 33% year-on-year in 2026, approximately 35% in 2027, and a four-year compound growth rate of approximately 29% from 2025 to 2029. At the same time, AI infrastructure construction will significantly drive up capital expenditures.

Goldman Sachs estimates that Amazon's cumulative capital expenditures from 2026 to 2028 will be approximately US$827 billion; the cost of each additional 1GW of data center capacity will be approximately US$50 billion to US$60 billion, of which US$15 billion will be infrastructure such as land and electricity, and US$35 billion to US$45 billion will be IT hardware such as servers and storage.

Amazon's data center capacity in 2025 will be about 6.85GW, and it plans to double it by 2027. Goldman Sachs estimates that AWS capital expenditures in 2027 may reach US$200 billion to US$250 billion.

In terms of capital requirements, Goldman Sachs predicts that high investment in AWS will cause Amazon's total free cash flow to have a gap of approximately US$70 billion from 2027 to 2028, of which FCFE may turn negative to US$41 billion in 2027.

However, the bank believes that Amazon's negative working capital model, higher cash reserves, low leverage and bond market financing capabilities are sufficient to cover the medium-term funding gap.

Goldman Sachs also said that Trainium 2 is "basically sold out" and Trainium 3 is "nearly fully booked." The annual revenue of self-developed chips is about 20 billion US dollars, and if sold to third parties, it can reach 50 billion US dollars.

The bank believes that Trainium will not replace Nvidia GPUs, but it is expected to reduce the cost of AI inference and become a driving force for AWS's long-term profit margin improvement. (

🔴 BREAKING — This directly extends this week’s Iran-ceasefire-collapse story with a crucial nuance: the crude oil crisis is stabilizing, but the products market — gasoline and diesel specifically — is genuinely tight and getting worse. US crude oil prices are up 5% to $73.90 since Trump’s renewed strikes, but gasoline futures jumped 6.1% and diesel over 10%, far outpacing crude’s move. The national average gas price hit $3.85 Thursday, up from $2.98 when the conflict began.

The core structural problem: US commercial crude inventories just posted their first weekly increase after 10 straight weeks of drawdowns, but the Strategic Petroleum Reserve sits at its lowest level since 1983, and Cushing, Oklahoma’s storage hub has hit operational limits that make further withdrawals difficult. Lipow Oil’s Andy Lipow’s warning is stark: “once the shelf is bare, there’s nowhere to turn” — meaning the only mechanism left to rebalance a real shortage would be demand destruction via higher prices. Kpler’s Matt Smith pinpoints the real mechanism at work: “we’ve lost, over the past four months, 10 million barrels a day out of the Middle East, and yet we’re at $70 a barrel” — offset almost entirely by refineries worldwide running fewer hours, not by genuine spare crude supply. Compounding this: Ukraine’s drone strikes have knocked out meaningful Russian refining capacity, Russia just banned diesel exports to protect domestic supply, and China is only now moving to lift its own product export ban.

💰 YOUR MOVE: This is the more important distinction than the crude-price panic occupying most headlines — refiners and fuel-distribution names may see real margin support from tight product markets even if crude itself stays range-bound, since gasoline and diesel cracks are widening faster than crude prices. Watch Gulf Coast gasoline inventories (7.2 million barrels below normal per OPIS) as the tightest, most immediate pressure point; if China’s product-export lift and rising US refinery runs don’t relieve this fast, expect continued outsized moves in retail fuel prices regardless of what crude itself does. This also complicates the “oil heading to $60” consensus from just last week — that thesis was built on a crude glut, but says nothing about the separate, now-binding constraint in refined products.

Hormuz is semi-closed. 90 targets struck in Iran. The IRGC is charging tolls to cross.

And crude fell 2% today.

How? Five reasons — and a lesson about how oil actually prices. 

1/ The data nobody on your timeline mentions: US crude inventories BUILT 3M barrels — first build since April.

Hormuz friction is 12,000 km away. The barrels in Cushing are here and now. Physical surplus beats distant scarcity, today.

2/ The supply cushion is massive: record Russian seaborne exports, US/Canada/Brazil growing, UAE pumping near-record post-OPEC exit.

Hormuz at 75% capacity removes ~1.7-2 mb/d. The cushion absorbs most of it. Without a FULL closure, the shock is manageable — and the market knows it.

3/ The subtle one: markets price BARRELS, not sovereignty.

Read the IRGC statement like a physical trader: Iran says traffic is recovering to 50% under ITS permit system. Iran wants the strait OPEN — it monetizes the toll, not the closure.

And the US is striking anti-ship capability while leaving Iran's energy infrastructure untouched.

Both sides are signaling: the barrels keep flowing

4/ Crude wasn't pricing "war yes/no." It was pricing a distribution where full closure had real weight.

Every day of surgical strikes + no energy targets + tolls instead of blockade NARROWS that tail. The premium attached to it gets refunded — even while visible friction (14 transits, dark shipping) gets worse.

Physical friction and price can diverge. They measure different things.

5/ Plus the macro headwind: the same energy shock feeds inflation → hawkish Fed → stronger dollar → lower crude.

Macro loops are never one-way.

6/ The lesson:

Markets trade the second derivative. Not "is there war?" but "is there MORE war than yesterday's price assumed?"

Today's answer was no. So the tape sold off while the strait stayed semi-closed.

Headlines measure drama. Price measures surprise.

NSE's Record Crude Derivatives Volumes: More Than Just a Trading Milestone

The Indian commodity derivatives market quietly crossed an important milestone this week.

On 9 July, the National Stock Exchange (NSE) recorded its highest-ever trading activity in crude oil derivatives, a sign that the market is becoming deeper, more liquid, and increasingly relevant for both institutional and retail participants.

The numbers tell the story.

A record 20.19 lakh trades were executed in crude derivatives, while the total traded value surged to ₹2,240.64 crore, comfortably surpassing the previous record of ₹1,662 crore. Overall trading volume touched 47.33 lakh contracts, with crude oil options accounting for 20.16 lakh trades and generating a premium turnover of ₹2,008.30 crore.

Perhaps the most significant number was NSE's 63.35% market share, highlighting its growing dominance in India's commodity derivatives ecosystem.

Why Are Crude Derivatives Seeing Such Strong Participation?

Crude oil is one of the world's most closely watched commodities. It directly influences inflation, transportation costs, manufacturing expenses, aviation, chemicals, paints, plastics, and even government finances.

In today's environment, crude prices are being driven by multiple global factors:

- Geopolitical tensions in key oil-producing regions.
- OPEC+ production decisions.
- Global demand expectations.
- US inventory data.
- Currency movements, particularly the US Dollar.

Every major move in crude oil creates both risk and opportunity. Derivatives provide market participants with an efficient way to hedge that risk or express a market view without owning the physical commodity.

Why Options Are Becoming the Preferred Choice

One of the standout trends is the rapid rise in crude oil options.

Unlike futures, options allow traders to define their maximum risk through the premium paid while still benefiting from price volatility. This makes them attractive not only for professional traders but also for sophisticated retail investors.

The sharp increase in options activity suggests that market participants are increasingly using structured strategies to manage uncertainty rather than relying solely on directional bets.

A Sign of a Maturing Market

Higher trading volumes are about more than just speculation.

Deep liquidity improves price discovery, reduces trading costs, narrows bid-ask spreads, and allows businesses to hedge commodity price risks more efficiently.

This is particularly important for sectors such as aviation, logistics, refining, chemicals, paints, plastics, and manufacturing, where fluctuations in crude prices can significantly impact profitability.

For institutional investors, greater liquidity also makes the market more attractive for deploying larger capital with lower execution risk.

The Bigger Picture

India is one of the world's largest importers of crude oil, making energy prices a critical driver of the country's economy. As India's financial markets continue to evolve, a vibrant commodity derivatives market becomes increasingly important for effective risk management and efficient capital allocation.

NSE's record trading volumes are therefore more than just a statistical achievement—they reflect growing confidence in India's commodity markets, increasing participation from both retail and institutional investors, and a market that is steadily becoming more sophisticated.

If this trend continues, commodity derivatives could play an even bigger role in India's financial ecosystem, providing better hedging tools, stronger liquidity, and more efficient price discovery for one of the economy's most important inputs.

Record trading volumes don't simply indicate higher activity—they often signal a market that is becoming deeper, smarter, and more resilient.

In 1983, NYMEX launched crude oil futures. Traders laughed. Oil was controlled by OPEC, not markets. Within a decade, oil futures became the most traded commodity contract on Earth.

In 2026, the Intercontinental Exchange is launching GPU compute futures.

Ornn, backed by Andreessen Horowitz with a $33 million seed round, built the Ornn Compute Price Index. It tracks live spot prices for NVIDIA's H100, H200, and B200 chips. The same hardware powering nearly every major AI model in production today.

The index has been live on Bloomberg Terminal since April 2026. In May, ICE partnered with Ornn to introduce USD cash-settled GPU compute futures based on the OCPI.

Goldman Sachs estimates $7.6 trillion in compute, power, and data center investments between 2026 and 2031. The financial infrastructure to sustain that spending does not exist yet. Ornn and ICE are building it.

Here is why this matters beyond trading desks. When an asset class gets futures contracts, three things happen.

First, price discovery. Everyone can see the real cost of compute, not just the companies that can afford it.

Second, hedging. Companies spending millions on GPU access can lock in prices and protect against spikes. This makes long-term AI planning feasible.

Third, speculation. Traders bet on compute demand the way energy traders bet on natural gas before winter.

Compute is the new oil. The futures markets are arriving. The companies that understand compute price risk will outlast the ones that do not.
